6 min read
Just now

--

Stack Overflow’s own traffic and survey data show a platform in decline and a trust gap in AI, here’s what that actually means for developers.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Introduction
Stack Overflow’s own query system tells a stark story: monthly question volume, which peaked above 200,000 in 2014, had fallen under 50,000 by late 2025 — back to roughly where it stood in the site’s first year. That’s not a slowdown. It’s close to a full round-trip.
The obvious read is that AI assistants replaced Stack Overflow. The actual data is more specific than that, and less settled. Stack Overflow’s own 2025 Developer Survey — 49,000+ respondents across 177 countries — shows AI adoption at an all-time high and trust in AI accuracy at a multi-year low, in the same dataset. That contradiction is the real answer to whether AI has replaced Stack Overflow, and it’s worth unpacking rather than rounding off into a headline.

What the Traffic Data Actually Shows
The decline predates ChatGPT. Question volume started dropping around 2014, coinciding with tighter moderation policies that closed and removed questions faster. That earlier decline is well documented by developers who track the platform closely, including engineers who’ve followed the site’s Data Explorer numbers directly.
What changed after November 2022 was the slope. Traffic that had been eroding gradually turned into a steep drop once ChatGPT launched, and the site’s monthly questions were sitting at 2008-launch levels by 2025. Stack Overflow’s parent company, Prosus, has been candid that this doesn’t map directly onto revenue — the platform’s business increasingly runs on enterprise products and data-licensing deals with AI companies rather than public-site ad traffic.
So there are really two separate stories tangled together: a community that was already cooling due to moderation friction, and a much sharper drop caused by developers getting answers inside their editor instead of a browser tab. Both are real. Neither fully explains the other.

What the Survey Data Adds
This is where the “AI replaced it” narrative gets more complicated. Stack Overflow’s 2025 Developer Survey found that 84% of developers now use or plan to use AI tools, up from 76% the year before. Adoption is not in question.
Trust is a different matter. The same survey found that only 29% of developers trust the accuracy of AI output, down from 40% the year before, while 46% actively distrust it — up sharply from 31% the prior year. Positive sentiment toward AI tools also fell, from over 70% in 2023–2024 to 60% in 2025.
The specific complaint driving this is concrete: the top frustration, cited by developers in the survey, is AI producing solutions that are “almost right, but not quite” — output plausible enough to look correct and wrong enough to cost real debugging time. A majority of respondents said they’re spending more time fixing AI-generated code because of exactly this pattern. And when asked what would make them still seek out a human in a future where AI handles most coding tasks, the top reason — cited by 75% of respondents — was simply not trusting AI’s answer.
That’s a striking pattern: usage and trust moving in opposite directions, which runs counter to how adoption curves for new technology usually behave. Normally, familiarity builds confidence. Here, more exposure to AI output appears to be making developers more aware of where it fails, not less.

So, Can It Replace Stack Overflow?
Based on the data, the honest answer splits into two parts.
For quick, common questions, largely yes. The kind of question that used to generate a duplicate-flagged Stack Overflow post — a syntax error, a common library usage pattern, a “why does this throw” — is exactly the category AI assistants handle inside the editor with less friction and no risk of a hostile moderator. That’s precisely the segment of traffic that collapsed.
For anything where correctness actually matters, not yet, by developers’ own account. The same survey population using AI daily is also the population reporting the lowest trust in its output on record, and citing “almost right” answers as their single biggest frustration. Stack Overflow’s own read on this — as a company with an obvious interest in the outcome, worth noting — is that there’s an emerging role for the platform as a human-verified reference specifically for checking AI-generated code, rather than as a first stop for questions.
There’s a selection-bias caveat worth stating plainly: the survey was fielded through Stack Overflow’s own channels, so its respondent pool skews toward developers still engaged enough with the platform to see the invitation. That doesn’t invalidate the trust numbers, but it means the sample may lean toward people more likely to value human verification in the first place.

What This Means in Practice

• Simple, well-defined questions are now genuinely faster to resolve with an AI assistant than with a forum post, and the traffic data confirms developers have already made that switch at scale.

• Complex or high-stakes code still gets treated with more skepticism by developers than the AI assistants producing it — verification, not blind trust, is the dominant behavior pattern the survey describes.

• The debugging tax is real and measured. A majority of developers report that fixing near-correct AI output now consumes time that used to go toward writing code directly — a cost worth accounting for when estimating AI’s net productivity effect.

• Community platforms aren’t disappearing, they’re repositioning. Stack Overflow’s own strategy shift — enterprise knowledge tools, data licensing, and framing itself as a verification layer for AI output — is a direct response to this same data.

Practical Checklist

• Use AI assistants for well-scoped, common questions where the answer is easy to verify quickly

• Apply extra scrutiny — and ideally a second source — for complex or unfamiliar problems, where “almost right” answers are most common

• Budget real time for verifying AI-generated code rather than assuming it’s ready to ship

• Keep a human-verified reference (documentation, community answers, code review) in the loop for anything security- or correctness-critical

• Treat declining trust scores as a signal to adjust workflow, not just a sentiment data point to note in passing

FAQ
Did AI actually cause Stack Overflow’s decline, or was it already happening? Both. Question volume was already falling from 2014 onward due to moderation changes, but the drop became much steeper after ChatGPT launched in November 2022.
If developers don’t trust AI’s accuracy, why do so many keep using it? The survey data suggests developers treat AI as a fast first draft rather than a final answer — useful for speed, but still requiring human verification, especially as tasks get more complex.
Is Stack Overflow actually going away? Its public question-and-answer traffic has fallen sharply, but the company has pivoted toward enterprise tools and data licensing, positioning itself as a verification source for AI-generated code rather than competing head-on with AI assistants for quick answers.
Does trust in AI improve as developers use it more? The opposite has happened in the most recent survey data — trust dropped even as usage rose, which the survey’s authors describe as inconsistent with typical technology adoption patterns.
What’s the biggest measurable cost of relying on AI-generated code? Time spent debugging output that’s close to correct but not quite — cited as the top frustration in Stack Overflow’s 2025 survey and linked directly to increased debugging time.

Conclusion
AI hasn’t replaced Stack Overflow so much as it’s absorbed the part of its traffic that never needed a community in the first place — the quick, common, easily verified question. What’s left is exactly the harder category: code where correctness matters enough that developers, by their own reported behavior, still don’t fully trust AI to get it right.
The data doesn’t support either extreme — neither “Stack Overflow is obsolete” nor “AI can’t be trusted for code.” It supports something more specific: use AI for speed, verify for correctness, and expect that gap to be the actual site of the debate for a while longer.

Sources

• Stack Overflow, 2025 Developer Survey: AI section — https://survey.stackoverflow.co/2025/ai/

• Stack Overflow, “Mind the gap: Closing the AI trust gap for developers” — https://stackoverflow.blog/2026/02/18/closing-the-developer-ai-trust-gap/
